chunk_summary: Indian adolescents face a double burden of malnutrition, with undernutrition and micronutrient deficiencies coexisting alongside rising obesity and cardiometabolic risks. Anemia, especially in girls, and deficiencies in vitamin D, folate, B12, and zinc are prevalent, requiring structured nutritional screening and intervention.
learning_objectives: Understand the double burden of malnutrition in Indian adolescents, including undernutrition and obesity.
Identify key micronutrient deficiencies such as anemia, vitamin D, folate, B12, and zinc in adolescents.
Recognize clinical signs of malnutrition and the importance of structured nutritional screening.
Analyze national survey data (CNNS, NFHS-5) to assess adolescent nutritional trends in India.
key_facts: Adolescence (10–19 years) is a period of high nutritional demand due to growth, lean mass gain, and menarche-related iron losses.
CNNS 2016–18 reports thinness (BMI-for-age <-2 SD) in about 25% of adolescents and overweight/obesity (BMI-for-age >+1 SD) as emerging concerns.
Anemia affects a majority of girls aged 15–19 years and about one-third of boys in the same age group (NFHS-5 data).
Micronutrient deficiencies in vitamin D, folate, B12, and zinc are common and often coexist.
Structured nutritional screening is essential as normal weight does not exclude micronutrient deficiencies.
reference_quote: CNNS 2016–18 findings in 10–19-year-olds report thinness (BMI-for-age <-2 SD) around one quarter, while overweight/obesity (BMI-for-age >+1 SD) is also present.
document_key_topics: Double burden of malnutrition in Indian adolescents
Structured nutritional screening and clinical signs
